meta.title: 6.4 Platform-Level Monitoring
meta.description: Build Make's platform health dashboard using five golden signals — preview success rate, mean time to preview, agent session completion, build pass rate, deployment success — and how error budgets turn reliability tradeoffs into math rather than organizational politics.

blocks.prompt:
It's Monday morning. You open your platform health dashboard before standup. What 5 charts tell you whether Make had a good weekend?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module gives you Make's five golden signals and the error budget framework that converts reliability decisions from organizational debates into arithmetic. By the end, you'll be able to read a Monday health dashboard, identify the only SLO breach requiring action, and explain to a non-engineer why "80% of budget consumed" means stopping deployments — not a preference, but a calculation.

Error budgets make the tradeoff between reliability and velocity a math problem instead of a political one — and that shift is the single most important thing a Platform PM can do to protect both product velocity and platform health simultaneously.

Platform-level monitoring is the view from altitude: not "what happened to user u_8842's session" but "how did the platform perform across all 50,000 sessions this weekend." This is the PM's primary interface with platform health — not because PMs should debug incidents, but because platform health metrics inform roadmap priorities, GA readiness decisions, and conversations with leadership.

---

**The Golden Signals for Make**

Google's Site Reliability Engineering book defines four "golden signals" for any service: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation. For Make, these translate into a platform-specific set:

**1. Preview Success Rate**
Of all preview loads attempted, what percentage succeeded (loaded fully, without errors, within the SLO threshold)?

This is your headline health metric. A drop here is the most user-visible failure. Target: 99.5%.

**2. Mean Time to Preview (MTTP)**
How long does it take from "user clicks Preview" to "preview is interactive in the browser"? Track p50, p95, and p99.

This is your primary user experience metric. Every 500ms added to MTTP is user patience consumed. Target: p95 under 3 seconds.

**3. Agent Session Completion Rate**
Of sessions where a user engages the agent with a goal, what percentage result in a deployed or exported output?

This is your efficacy metric — not just whether the platform ran, but whether it helped users accomplish something. Target: track and trend; baseline before optimizing.

**4. Build Pass Rate (First Attempt)**
Of build attempts, what percentage succeed without the user needing to request a fix?

This is your code quality proxy. A declining build pass rate often precedes user satisfaction decline because users experience failed builds as the platform "not working."

**5. Deployment Success Rate**
Of deployment attempts, what percentage result in a live, accessible URL?

This is the end-of-funnel metric. It captures failures in the CDN layer, DNS propagation, and deployment pipeline that preview success rate doesn't.

---

**SLOs and Error Budgets**

Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are internal reliability targets. They're not marketing claims — they're engineering contracts that determine when platform work takes priority over feature work.

Example SLOs for Make:
- Preview success rate: 99.5% over any 30-day window
- p95 preview load time: under 3 seconds
- Agent session completion rate: above 68% (hypothetical baseline)
- Deployment success rate: 99.0%

An **error budget** is the inverse of your SLO. If your preview success rate SLO is 99.5%, you have a 0.5% error budget — you can afford to fail 0.5% of preview attempts per month without violating your commitment.

Error budgets make reliability tradeoffs concrete. If you've consumed 80% of your preview error budget on the 20th of the month, you stop shipping changes to the preview stack for the rest of the month. If your budget is healthy, you can ship faster. This is how reliability and velocity get reconciled without politics — the math decides.

blocks.question: Your preview success rate SLO is 99.5%. It's the 20th of the month and you've consumed 80% of your error budget. A feature team wants to ship a preview stack change that carries a 0.2% risk of introducing a preview failure regression. Should you approve the deployment?
blocks.answer: No. With 80% of the budget consumed and 10 days remaining, you have 0.1% of budget left (0.5% total × 20% remaining). The proposed change carries a 0.2% regression risk — twice the remaining budget. Approving it risks breaching the SLO commitment for the month. The right answer is to defer the deployment to next month's budget cycle, or to require additional mitigation (feature flag, canary deployment to 1% of traffic) that reduces the regression risk below 0.1%. The error budget framework converts this from a debate into arithmetic.

blocks.content:
---

**Alerting Tiers**

Not every anomaly warrants waking someone at 2am. Alerting tiers prevent alert fatigue:

| Tier | Trigger | Response |
|---|---|---|
| Page (PagerDuty) | Preview success rate drops below 97%, sustained 5 minutes | On-call engineer woken immediately |
| Slack alert | Build pass rate drops more than 5% from 24h baseline | Team notified during business hours |
| Dashboard ticket | p95 preview load time trending up for 3 consecutive days | Added to Monday review, prioritized for next sprint |

The threshold for paging is deliberately conservative. Broad alerts on minor anomalies destroy on-call morale and cause engineers to start ignoring pages — the opposite of what you want.

---

**Dashboards by Audience**

Build separate dashboards for separate audiences, or you'll build one dashboard that serves no one well:

**Engineering on-call dashboard:** Infrastructure metrics, active incidents, service error rates, deployment pipeline status. Refreshes every 30 seconds.

**Product team dashboard:** Feature adoption funnels, session completion rates, agent success rates by template type, build failure reasons. Refreshes every hour. This is the PM's primary tool.

**Leadership dashboard:** Weekly trend lines for key quality metrics, cost per session, user growth vs. infrastructure cost, SLO compliance over the quarter. Refreshes daily.

---

**Anomaly Detection**

Threshold-based alerts catch acute spikes. Anomaly detection catches gradual regressions that never cross a single threshold.

Two patterns worth building into Make's monitoring:

**Sudden anomaly:** Build failure rate jumps from 8% to 22% within 30 minutes. Almost certainly an external dependency (npm registry outage, a popular package publishing a broken version). Detected by rate-of-change alerting.

**Gradual regression:** p95 preview load time increases from 2.1s to 2.8s over three weeks. No single day's change is significant enough to trigger an alert. Detected by trend analysis with a linear regression against a 14-day baseline.

Gradual regressions are the more dangerous failure mode because they're invisible to threshold alerting. Building trend-based anomaly detection is a platform investment that pays off disproportionately.

## How to Think About This

The error budget is a pre-commitment mechanism. Without it, the reliability vs. velocity debate happens in every meeting, at the mercy of whoever argues most persuasively. With it, the debate happened once — when the SLO was set — and every subsequent deployment decision is just arithmetic. Think of it as a monthly reliability allowance: you can spend it how you choose, but once it's gone, it's gone. The asymmetry is intentional: it's easy to say "let's ship this feature, reliability is fine" in the moment; it's harder to argue with a number that says you have 0.1% of your budget left.

---

blocks.question: Your Monday dashboard shows: preview success rate 99.6% (green), p95 MTTP 3.8 seconds (red, SLO is 3s), build pass rate 91% (green), deployment success rate 99.2% (green). What do you do first?
blocks.answer:
Investigate the p95 MTTP violation. It's the only SLO breach. Pull traces from the slowest previews in the last 72 hours to identify the bottleneck. Check if the trend is worsening or stable. This is your standup talking point and your first engineering ask.

---

blocks.title:
Look up the Figma status page (status.figma.com). Observe the structure: which services are listed, how incidents are categorized, how communication is structured. Now imagine Make's status page. What services would you list? What's your incident communication template for a preview outage?

---
blocks.solution: On Figma's status page you'll find service components (Editor, Prototyping, File Access, etc.) with individual status indicators, a history of recent incidents, and incident communications that progress from 'Investigating' to 'Identified' to 'Resolved' with timestamps. For Make's equivalent, you'd list: Preview Environment, Agent (Code Generation), Build Pipeline, Deployment / CDN, and Authentication. An incident communication template for a preview outage might read: 'Investigating — [TIME]: We are investigating reports of preview failures. Some users may see blank or slow previews. We will update in 30 minutes.' → 'Identified — [TIME]: We have identified a degraded npm registry connection causing dependency install timeouts. We are routing around the affected registry endpoint.' → 'Resolved — [TIME]: Preview success rate has returned to normal. Affected sessions: approximately [N]. Root cause and follow-up actions will be shared in our incident report within 48 hours.' The structure matters: every update should state current status, what you know, and when the next update comes — never leave users with a stale 'Investigating' update for more than 30 minutes.

blocks.prompt:
Explain the concept of an error budget to a product manager who has no engineering background. Use an analogy outside of software. Explain why "we've consumed 80% of our error budget" means "stop shipping new features to this service."

---

questions: Think of a product or platform you've managed or used professionally that had a formal reliability target. Was it an SLO, an SLA, or an informal agreement? What happened when the target was at risk — did the team self-enforce a feature freeze, or did reliability debt accumulate silently?

Recall a time you were on a team that was shipping fast but accumulating reliability risk. What was the trigger that finally forced the conversation about slowing down? Looking back, what monitoring signal, if it had existed, would have surfaced that risk weeks earlier?

In AI-powered products you've studied or built, what are the equivalent 'golden signals' to the four SRE signals — something richer than uptime and latency that captures whether the AI is actually doing its job? Do those signals typically exist as dashboards, or does the team find out through support tickets?

---

blocks.content:
Platform health for Make centers on five golden signals — preview success rate, mean time to preview, agent session completion rate, build pass rate, and deployment success rate — and error budgets make the tradeoff between reliability and velocity a math problem instead of a political one.

---

blocks.prompt:
You've covered what to monitor and how to alert on it. But infrastructure costs $2M/month and leadership wants to know where the money goes. Next: cost observability.

---

reviewQuestions.question: What are Make's five golden signals for platform health, as defined in the module?
options: CPU utilization, memory pressure, network latency, disk I/O, and container restarts
Preview success rate, mean time to preview (MTTP), agent session completion rate, build pass rate (first attempt), and deployment success rate
DAU growth, session length, retention rate, NPS, and support ticket volume
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module defines Make's five golden signals as: (1) Preview success rate — headline health metric, target 99.5%; (2) Mean time to preview — primary UX metric, target p95 under 3 seconds; (3) Agent session completion rate — efficacy metric; (4) Build pass rate on first attempt — code quality proxy; (5) Deployment success rate — end-of-funnel metric.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Solid recall! Those five signals cover the full funnel from preview health through deployment.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: CPU and memory are Layer 1 infrastructure signals. DAU and NPS are product/business metrics. Make's platform-specific golden signals focus on the core user journey: can users get a preview, does the agent help them build something, and can they deploy it?

reviewQuestions.question: If a preview success rate SLO is 99.5% and 80% of the error budget is consumed by the 20th of the month, how much budget remains?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The error budget is 0.5% (100% - 99.5% SLO). If 80% of that budget is consumed, 20% remains: 0.5% × 20% = 0.1% remaining. The module uses this exact calculation to show that a proposed change carrying 0.2% regression risk would exceed the remaining budget and should be deferred.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the math — 0.5% total budget, 20% remaining = 0.1% left. Any change with greater than 0.1% regression risk should be deferred.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The error budget is the inverse of the SLO: 100% - 99.5% = 0.5% total budget. With 80% consumed, 20% of 0.5% remains — which is 0.1%, not 0.4% or 1.0%. This is the calculation that makes reliability decisions mechanical rather than political.

reviewQuestions.question: Which alerting tier in the module's framework warrants waking an on-call engineer immediately via PagerDuty?
options: p95 preview load time trending upward for 3 consecutive days
Build pass rate dropping more than 5% from the 24-hour baseline
Preview success rate dropping below 97% sustained for 5 minutes
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's alerting tier table shows: preview success rate below 97% for 5 sustained minutes triggers an immediate PagerDuty page. A 5%+ build pass rate drop triggers a Slack alert during business hours. A multi-day p95 trend creates a dashboard ticket for Monday review. The paging threshold is deliberately conservative to protect on-call morale.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — preview success rate below 97% for 5 minutes is the PagerDuty threshold. That's a significant, sustained user-visible failure.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Multi-day p95 trends go to the Monday dashboard review queue, and build pass rate drops go to a Slack team alert. Only the preview success rate dropping below 97% for 5 sustained minutes triggers an immediate page — it's the most user-visible failure mode and warrants waking someone up.
